coredata.dc:title: Novel Fluorene-based compounds: Investigation on the synthesis, function and fluorescence mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xin-Yue
Hui, Tian-Qi
Han, Ying-Ying
Huang, Xin-Tong
Jiang, Xiao-Nan
Liu, Cui
Yan, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Fluorene-based compounds are known for their unique role in photoluminescence and electroluminescent materials. Based on this idea, a series of fluorene-based fluorescent compounds were designed and synthesized by the Suzuki coupling reaction. The luminescence effect was evaluated by comparing fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield of the control compounds. The research indicated that the novel fluorene-based compounds 2,7-di(quinol-4-yl)-9,9-dimethylfluorene(F4) and 2,7-di(indole-5-yl)-9,9-dimethyl fluorene (F5) have better fluorescence characteristics. They are used as sensors for the recognition of nitro compounds and metal ions. In addition F4 has good selectivity and sensitivity to TNP(160600 M−1, 2.63 μM) and Fe3+ (121140 M−1, 1.68 μM). Finally, fluorescence quenching mechanism of F4 with TNP was studied by theoretical calculation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent
Fluorene compounds
2,4,6-trinitrophenol
Fe3+
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Limit of detection and calculation of Stern-Volmer constant
Determination of detection limits and sensitivity
The sensing properties to metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for support provided by the Basic Natural Science Research Project of Liaoning Education Department (Grant No. LJ2019008 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence spectra of fluorene and compounds F1-F7.(the fluorescence spectra of F6 was using DMF as a solvent, and the fluorescence spectra of other compounds was using ethanol as a solvent).
Emission spectra of sensors in the presence of various concentrations of TNP((A)F4, (B)F5, up to 100 μM).
The column graph of reduction in fluorescence intensity(plotted as quenching efficiency) upon the addition of different analytes.
Stern-Volmer plots of analytes in higher concentration ranges of analytes for sensors((A)F4, (B)F5, up to 100 μM).
S-V diagram of sensor when the concentration of NACs is low ((A)F4,up to 40 μm (B)F5, up to 50 μm).
Normalized curve of the intensity of sensors with concentration((A)F4 (B)F5).
Fluorescence quenching efficiency upon addition of different nitro compounds followed by TNP.
Partial 1H NMR spectra of sensors and upon addition of TNP in DMSO‑d6 .
HOMO and LUMO energy orbitals of sensor F4 with TNP, calculated by using at DFT method and B3LYP/6–31G(d) basis set.
Fluorescence response of sensors to different metal ions((A) F4 (B) F5).
The column graph of reduction in fluorescence intensity (plotted as quenching efficiency) upon the addition of different analytes.
Emission spectra of sensors in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+((A) F4 (B) F5).
Fluorescence excitation and emission of fluorescence sensors.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield using ethanol as a standard.

head.ce:title: Novel Fluorene-based compounds: Investigation on the synthesis, function and fluorescence mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Huanghe Road 850#, Dalian 116029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Huanghe Road 850#, Dalian 116029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorene-based compounds are known for their unique role in photoluminescence and electroluminescent materials. Based on this idea, a series of fluorene-based fluorescent compounds were designed and synthesized by the Suzuki coupling reaction. The luminescence effect was evaluated by comparing fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield of the control compounds. The research indicated that the novel fluorene-based compounds 2,7-di(quinol-4-yl)-9,9-dimethylfluorene(F4) and 2,7-di(indole-5-yl)-9,9-dimethyl fluorene (F5) have better fluorescence characteristics. They are used as sensors for the recognition of nitro compounds and metal ions. In addition F4 has good selectivity and sensitivity to TNP(160600 M−1, 2.63 μM) and Fe3+ (121140 M−1, 1.68 μM). Finally, fluorescence quenching mechanism of F4 with TNP was studied by theoretical calculation.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probe is a special type of sensor that has attracted attention in the field of analytical sensing and optical imaging due to its high sensitivity, fast response time, and simple technology [1,2]. And fluorescent-based compounds have strong electron conjugation, which means large electron delocalization and high fluorescence efficiency [3]. In recent years, anthraquinones [4,5], pyrene derivatives [6,7], biphenyls [8], styrenes [9], imidazoles [10] and polyurethane [11,12] become the main raw materials of fluorescent probe. Fluorene is a rigid plane consisting of a five-membered ring connecting two benzene rings. Their derivatives are known for their unique role in photoluminescence and electroluminescent materials due to the inclusion of biphenyl units and physical properties [13–17]. Our team has been interested in the study of fluorene compounds [16,18,19].

In our previous work, 4,4-(9,9-dimethylfluorene-2,7-diyl)dibenzoic acid(F7) was synthesized from fluorene as a raw material through bromination, methylation reaction, Suzuki coupling reaction and hydrolysis reaction [18]. Compared with fluorene, F7 is a conjugation of fluorene and a benzene ring, which enhances fluorescence and a red shift occurs in the maximum emission wavelength. After that, 2,7-bis-(4‑tert‑butyl‑phenyl)−9,9-dimethyl-9H-fluoren(F6) was synthesized by the suzuki coupling reaction of 2,7-dibromo-9,9-dimethyl-9H-fluorene and 4‑tert-butylphenylboronicacid [19]. The experiments indicated that the fluorescence intensity of F6 was slightly higher than F7. It is speculated that carboxyl group is electron-withdrawing group and tert‑butyl group is electron-donating group, which is the main reason for the difference of fluorescence intensity. Since the fluorescence intensity is related to many factors, such as the length of the conjugated chain and the solvent. In order to further understand the effect of fluorene-linked groups on their fluorescence characteristics, 2,7-di(quinol-4-yl)−9,9-dimethylfluorene(F4) and2,7-di (indole-5-yl)−9,9-dimethyl fluorene(F5) were designed and synthesized by introducing quinoline and indole.

The selective detection of lethal explosive materials is decisive for homeland security, industrial and environmental safety and human health. Explosive compounds are mainly nitroaromatic hydrocarbons [20–24]. Among them, TNP has high explosive power and is widely used in rocket fuel, military fighters, fireworks and industry. Due to its high acidity and good solubility in water, TNP is also a notorious environmental pollutant for irrigated land and groundwater supplies [25–28]. Therefore, It's absolutely essential to detect NACs quickly, sensitively and selectively.

The development of fluorescent sensors has received considerable attention for metal ions. Metal ions induce many environmental issues, such as in plant, soil, and waste water. Iron(Fe) is one of the most abundant elements on the earth and is indispensable for the survival of animals and plants and industrial production [29–33]. It plays a vital role in cell metabolism, enzyme catalysis, oxygen transport in hemoglobin, and as a cofactor in enzyme-based reactions. Excessive and insufficient Fe3+ can lead to serious diseases [23,34,35]. Therefore, a lot of work has been done especially on monitoring and detecting of Fe3+ in plants, soil and waste water [36,37,38].

For this purpose, we synthesized a series of fluorene compounds, F4 and F5 are novel compounds that have not been reported in literature. They are used as sensors for the recognition of nitro compounds and metal ions. F4 has good selectivity and sensitivity to TNP(160,600 M−1, 2.63 μM) and Fe3+ (121,140 M−1, 1.68 μM). Compared with the identification of TNP and Fe3+ in the literature, it has the advantages of high sensitivity, low detection limit and strong anti-interference ability [39–41]. At the same time, the quenching mechanism of TNP was further determined by theoretical calculation and nuclear magnetic titration.

4-bromoquinoline and reagents were purchased from commercial sources and used without further purification. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were measured on a Bruker AV 500 spectrometer in deuterated chloroform or dichloromethane using tetramethylsilane (TMS;δ = 0) as the internal reference. Fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000) recorded the spectrum. For all fluorescence studies, the fluorescence experiments have slit widths of 5 nm(excitation) and 5 nm(emission).

The synthetic routes of fluorene-based compounds are shown in Scheme 1 . Among them, 2,7-Dibromofluorene(F1), 2,7-Dibromo-9,9-dimethylfluorene(F2), 2,2-(9,9-dimethyl-9H-fluorene-2,7-diyl)bis(4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane)(F3), 2,7-bis-(4‑tert‑butyl‑phenyl)−9,9-dimethyl-9H-fluoren(F6) and 4,4-(9,9-dimethylfluorene-2,7-diyl)dibenzoic acid (F7) were synthesized by the reported procedures [16,17]. Synthetic details of these compounds (F1), (F2), (F3), (F6) and (F7) are mentioned in the supporting information. Fortunately, we obtained the compounds 2,7-di(quinol-4-yl)−9,9-dimethyl fluorene(F4) and 2,7-di(indole-5-yl)−9,9-dimethyl fluorene(F5) by Suzuki coupling reaction, which were not in any literature before. The following is the specific synthesis method and the characterization of F4 and F5.
2,7-di(quinol-4-yl)−9,9-dimethyl fluorene(F4):

The mixtures of F3 (4.46 g, 10 mmol), 4-bromoquinoline(4.16 g, 20 mmol), Pd(PPh3)4 (1.00 g, 0.45 mmol), K3PO4(5.1 g, 96 mmol), and N,N-dimethylformamide (50 mL) were added to a flask. The flask was equipped with a water condenser and stirred at 110 °C under nitrogen for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, 250 mL of water was added and then extracted with CH2Cl2 (3 × 50 mL); the combined organic layer was washed with saturated saline and dried over anhydrous MgSO4. The solvent was evaporated and the residue was purified by column chromatography over silica gel using ethyl acetate/petroleum ether (1:5, v/v) to get white solid(3.37 g, 75%). 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3), δ(TMS, ppm): 8.98 (d, J = 4.4 Hz, 2H), 8.22 (d, J = 8.2 Hz, 2H), 8.05–7.99 (m, 2H), 7.94 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 2H), 7.79–7.71 (m, 2H), 7.62 (d, J = 1.2 Hz, 2H), 7.57–7.51 (m, 4H), 7.45 (t, J = 4.8 Hz, 2H), 1.62 (s, 6H). 13C NMR (125 MHz, CDCl3), δ(TMS, ppm): 155.79 (s), 151.21 (s), 150.45 (s), 150.02 (s), 140.39 (s), 138.80 (s), 131.23 (s), 130.89 (s), 130.29 (s), 128.26 (d, J = 15.0 Hz), 127.32 (s), 125.49 (s), 122.82 (s), 121.82 (s), 8.74 (s), 28.59 (s).
2,7-di(indole-5-yl)−9,9-dimethyl fluorene (F5):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection and calculation of Stern-Volmer constant
ce:section.ce:para: The detection limit of the TNP sensor was calculated using the following eqn: detection limit = 3δ/k. Herein, δ is the standard deviation of the blank measurements and the k is the slope of the calibration curve.

The Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated according to the Stern-Volmer equation: I0/I = Ksv[Q] + 1, where I0 and I are fluorescence intensity of sensor before and after exposuring to the nitro compound; [Q] is the quencher concentration.

As shown in Fig. 1
and Table 1
, The maximum emission wavelength was obtained by fluorescence spectra of fluorene compounds and their fluorescence quantum yield was measured. The larger emission wavelength (399 nm, 392 nm) and the higher fluorescence quantum yield (0.572, 0.436), resulted in stronger fluorescence intensity of F4 and F5. However, the maximum emission wavelength of F7 is larger than F6. We speculate that DMF with greater polarity as a solvent when measuring the fluorescence performance of F7, which caused the maximum emission wavelength and exhibited bathochromic shift. However, the fluorescence properties of the other compounds were measured using ethanol as solvent. Because of fluorescent dyes with large stokes shift have the advantages of low background interference, small light damage to biological samples, strong sample penetration, and high detection sensitivity. Therefore, we calculated the stokes shift of three compounds in combination with fluorescence characteristics to analyze four fluorene compounds F4-F7. Finally, we believe that F4 and F5 with larger stokes shift (79 nm, 72 nm) have good development prospect. Therefore, we apply F4 and F5 to the identification of nitro compounds and metal ions.

The 10 μM solution of the sensors F4 and F5 was prepared using ethanol as a solvent. Different concentrations of NACs were added and fluorescence emission spectras were measured. As depicted in Fig. 2 , after adding TNP, the luminescence intensity of sensors were significantly reduced. While other analytes do not show negligible changes in fluorescence emission spectrum. And the change in fluorescence intensity with other analytes is provided in the support information(Fig. S13, Fig. S14).

As shown in Fig. 3 , when TNP concentration was 100 μM, the most effective quencher for F4 and F5 is TNP. And the quenching efficiency is 97% and 86% for F4 and F5. Other NACs can reduce the photoluminescence intensity of the sensors to different level. In all cases, the initial fluorescence intensity of the sensors decreased significantly as NACs gradually added. The quenching rate is essentially related to the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv), indicating that TNP has faster fluorescence quenching than other nitroaromatic compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of detection limits and sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para:
To better understand this fact, the Stern-Volmer constant of the quenching process was calculated. As shown in Fig. 4
, the linear ratio for (I0/I-1) was observed at low concentrations of all nitro analytes. The Stern-Volmer pattern deviates linearly begins to bend upward as the concentration of the nitroaromatic compound increases. Linear changes at low concentrations are mainly due to static quenching, while steep curves at higher concentrations may be due to dynamic quenching. Static quenching can be attributed to ground state interactions between the analyte and the sensor. Dynamic quenching is primarily due to the energy and electron transfer process between the analytes and the F4 and F5.

The normalized curve of F4 and F5 allows the determination of the quenching constant as shown in Fig. 5
. The larger Ksv values revealed extremely high sensitivity. For F4, the Ksv value of TNP is 160,600 M−1. For F5, the Ksv value of TNP is 54,890 M−1. The Ksv values of other NACs are given in Table S1. Therefore, the F4 becomes one of the best candidates for the detection of TNP.

In order to prove the sensors' ability to detect TNP, we plot the normalized curve of the luminescence intensity with concentration as shown in Fig. 6
. The detection limits of F4 and F5 are calculated to be 2.63 and 3.77 μM for TNP, respectively. Therefore, it is further explained that F4 can better detect TNP.

In addition, by measuring the Stern-Volmer constant, fluorescence quenching was mainly caused by charge transfer. To further determine the formation of charge transfer, TNP was added to DMSO‑d6 solution of sensor F4, and immediately the 1H NMR measurement was performed immediately. As shown in the Fig. 8
, the chemical shifts of the protons on the fluorene ring of F4 were shifted to the low field after the TNP was added, which indicates that the electron cloud density on the fluorene ring is reduced by the influence of TNP. It is proved that there is electron transfer between the TNP and F4. Experiments have shown that the chemical protons of the hydrogen protons on the annulus around F4 have a strong chemical shift, which strongly proves that the fluorescence quenching of the sensors is an electron transfer mechanism.

In order to further study the selective origin of sensor for TNP, the quenching mechanism was studied. Using the Gaussian 09 program through density functional theory to study the HOMO-LUMO level of TNP and F4. In terms of optimization, the B3LYP function of the 6–31G(d) base set is used. The fluorescence quenching process may be produced by photoelectron transfer (PET). In order to verify PET, electrons are first excited from the electron-rich HOMO of the F4 to its LUMO, and then transferred to the LUMO of the electron-defective TNP causing fluorescence quenching. The energy gap between the LUMO of the donor fluorophore (F4) and the acceptor TNP is the primary driving force for these transitions. As shown in the Fig. 9
, the energy level diagrams of F4, [F4+TNP], and TNP showed that in the presence of TNP, the initial band gap of F4 is 4.08 eV, and after forming a complex, the band gap is reduced to 3.00 eV. Further, the LUMO energy (1.63 eV) of the F4 is lower than the LUMO energy of [F4 + TNP] (2.99 eV). These evidences strongly proved that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of F4 is electron transfer mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing properties to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
Various cations were tested in parallel under the same conditions for evaluating the specific sensing behavior of the F4 and F5, upon the addition of other ions such as Fe3+, Pb2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Cd2+ ( Fig. 10
(A),(B)). Excitingly, The results showed that sensors displayed an extremely good selectivity for Fe3+, instead of the other ions. We speculate that reduction of the luminous intensity is due to the absorption of the energy of the excitation light by Fe3+ [42,43].

As shown in Fig. 11
, the degree of fluorescence quenching was found to be different after the addition of different cations to the sensor. From the quenching efficiency figure can be seen that Fe3+ has the best quenching effect on F4, F5. And the Ksv of Fe3+ for sensors F4 and F5 were calculated to be 121,140 and 24,750 M−1, respectively (As shown in Fig. S15). The detection limits of sensors F4, F5 for Fe3+ were calculated to be 1.68 and 5.88 μM (As shown in Fig. 12
(A),(B)), respectively. This proved that sensor F4 is an effective sensor for Fe3+.

In summary, a series of fluorene-based fluorescent compounds were designed and synthesized. The novel fluorescence sensors F4, F5 with large stokes shift were obtained by fluorescence spectrum and fluorescence quantum yield. They are used in the detection of nitro compounds and metal ions. Experimental results showed that F4 has higher sensitivity and lower detection limit for the detection of TNP and Fe3+. The theoretical calculation and nuclear magnetic titration proved that the mechanism of electron transfer fluorescence quenching between F4 and TNP. We hope the unique physical and chemical properties can also help to explore some new semiconductors and sensing materials.

Xin-Yue Zhou: Writing - original draft, Software. Tian-Qi Hui: Writing – review & editing. Ying-Ying Han: Writing – review & editing. Xin-Tong Huang: Conceptualization. Xiao-Nan Jiang: Validation. Cui Liu: Validation. Jie Yan: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for support provided by the Basic Natural Science Research Project of Liaoning Education Department (Grant No. LJ2019008).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2021.130185.
